entry.title: Generative AI with SAP – Introduction to SAP Business AI
entry.content:
In today’s fast-evolving digital economy, businesses are increasingly seeking ways to enhance productivity, improve decision-making, and deliver personalized experiences. Artificial Intelligence (AI) has emerged as a cornerstone in this transformation, and SAP is at the forefront, embedding AI directly into its business processes through SAP Business AI.

This blog serves as an introduction to SAP Business AI, with a focus on Generative AI and its integration into the SAP ecosystem.

 

What Is SAP Business AI?

SAP Business AI is SAP’s portfolio of AI technologies and services embedded across its applications and platforms. Unlike generic AI platforms, SAP Business AI is enterprise-ready, context-aware, and responsible by design, tailored to help businesses become more intelligent and agile.

Key components of SAP Business AI include:

1.Embedded AI capabilities in SAP applications (e.g., SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Customer Experience)

2.Industry-specific AI scenario

  1. Business-centric AI models trained on anonymized and consented SAP data
  2. Tools for developing, deploying, and managing AI

 

What Is Generative AI?

Generative AI refers to AI systems capable of generating new content, such as text, images, code, and even designs. It leverages large language models (LLMs) and transformers to understand and produce human-like outputs based on prompts.

In a business context, Generative AI opens up possibilities for:

  1. Automating document creation (e.g., invoices, job descriptions)
  2. Summarizing reports and meeting notes
  3. Writing personalized marketing content
  4. Assisting in coding and debugging
  5. Enhancing customer service with intelligent chatbots

 

Generative AI in SAP Business AI

SAP integrates Generative AI within its solutions through a responsible and enterprise-focused approach.

  1. SAP Joule – Your AI Copilot

SAP introduced Joule; a natural language copilot embedded across SAP cloud applications. Joule helps users:

  1. Retrieve business insights in real time
  2. Automate tasks with simple prompts
  3. Interact conversationally with enterprise data

For example, a procurement officer can ask:
What are the top 5 vendors based on last quarter’s delivery performance?
Joule delivers a contextual, data-driven answer instantly.

  1. Partnering with Leading LLM Providers

SAP collaborates with OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google Cloud Vertex AI to integrate advanced LLMs into its applications, while maintaining enterprise-grade data privacy and security.

  1. SAP AI Core and AI Launchpad

For developers, SAP AI Core provides infrastructure to manage and deploy AI models, including Generative AI. SAP AI Launchpad offers tools to monitor and govern these models responsibly.

 

Use Cases of Generative AI in SAP

Here are some real-world examples of how Generative AI is used across SAP solutions:

AreaUse Case
HR (SuccessFactors)Auto-generate job descriptions based on role requirements
Finance (S/4HANA)Summarize financial reports and detect anomalies
Customer ServiceGenerate dynamic responses in chatbots
ProcurementDraft vendor emails and contracts
Sales & MarketingCreate personalized campaign content

Responsible AI with SAP

SAP emphasizes ethical AI use, focusing on:

  1. Transparency: Clearly informing users when AI is used
  2. Privacy: Ensuring data is handled securely and anonymized
  3. Fairness: Avoiding bias in model training and outputs

With its AI Ethics Policy and governance frameworks, SAP is committed to delivering AI that customers can trust.

 

Getting Started

To explore SAP Business AI:

  1. Try out Joule in your SAP applications
  2. Explore the SAP AI Core and Launchpad on SAP BTP
  3. Visit the SAP Discovery Center for Generative AI use cases
  4. Join the SAP Community to connect with AI experts and peers

Generative AI is more than a buzzword - it’s a powerful tool driving intelligent automation and innovation across industries. With SAP Business AI, organizations can unlock the value of AI within familiar business processes, responsibly and securely.

Stay tuned for deep dives into specific use cases, technical integrations, and customer success stories in future posts.

 

What Is Joule?

Imagine having a trusted AI companion by your side, ready to assist and enhance your daily operations using the power of natural language and generative capabilities across SAP’s cloud portfolio that’s Joule.

Joule is:

  1. An advanced, generative AI copilot embedded throughout SAP’s cloud enterprise portfolio
  2. Designed to deliver proactive and contextualized insights from SAP applications and third-party sources
  3. Built to help people get work done faster and drive better business outcomes in a secure, compliant way

 

How Will Customers Use Joule?

Joule quickly sorts through and contextualizes data from multiple systems to surface smarter insights. It will be embedded across applications including HR, finance, supply chain, procurement, and customer experience, and integrated into SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

Users can ask questions or present problems in plain language and receive intelligent, context-aware answers.

For example:

  1. A manufacturer asks Joule about sales performance.
  2. Joule identifies underperforming regions, links it to supply chain data, and suggests fixes.
  3. In HR, it can write inclusive job descriptions and suggest interview questions.

 

Some Important Facts About Joule

Integration with SAP Applications:
Joule provides a conversational UI integrated with SAP apps, using SAP Fiori-compliant components.

Enterprise-Readiness:
Out-of-the-box integration with SAP backend systems, aligned with AI ethics, GDPR, and privacy controls. Joule is SOC-II compliant.

Automatic Updates:
Joule is continuously updated to include new capabilities and improvements.

 

How to Stay Updated on Joule

Explore these resources:

  1. SAP Joule product page
  2. SAP Community for Joule updates
  3. SAP Discovery Center for use cases

 

Joule in Procurement – Empowering Smart, AI-Driven Decisions

Procurement is a strategic function where speed, accuracy, and insight are critical. Joule brings transformative capabilities to procurement by enabling:

  1. Conversational access to enterprise data
  2. Automation of repetitive tasks
  3. Enhanced decision-making

Real-Time Spend Analysis

Instead of digging through reports, simply ask -
"What was our total spend on IT hardware in Q1 across all regions?"
Joule retrieves and visualizes the data instantly.

Supplier Evaluation

Ask:
Which suppliers had the highest late delivery rate last quarter?
Joule evaluates performance using embedded analytics and ML.

RFQ and Email Drafting

Joule can:

  1. Draft RFQs
  2. Create follow-up emails
  3. Propose contract language

Example:
Draft an RFQ for 500 ergonomic office chairs with delivery in two weeks.

Workflow Acceleration

Speed up approvals:

  1. Show PRs needing my approval
  2. Approve all low-risk PRs below $5,000

Compliance & Risk Detection

Stay audit-ready:
Which POs are missing documentation?
Joule flags issues proactively.

 

Why It Matters

In procurement, Joule enables:

  1. Faster decision-making
  2. Improved collaboration
  3. Proactive risk mitigation
  4. Higher operational efficiency

Joule doesn’t replace professionals — it amplifies them.

Coming soon: Expanded capabilities across the entire procurement lifecycle — from sourcing to supplier collaboration — all within the trusted SAP ecosystem.

 

https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-assistant.html

 

Thankyou...

entry.title: Reimagining Decision-Making: AI as the Catalyst for Unbiased Strategies
entry.content:
Context and Problem

In today’s dynamic business landscape, decisions drive growth, innovation, and transformation. Yet even the most seasoned leaders aren’t immune to cognitive biases(mental shortcuts) or struggle with missing/delayed/overwhelming information which then leads to flawed judgments. From the sunk cost fallacy to conformity bias, from too-limited to too-generic data; unseen forces can result in costly inefficiencies, missed opportunities, and organizational missteps. 

In our recent user research,  >60 professionals' responses were collected via survey designed to explore decision-making patterns within teams at B2B companies. Participants were asked to reflect on their teams’ behaviours in workplace scenarios. These behaviours included relying on heuristics (e.g., availability and anchoring), incorporating probability, reacting to new information, and navigating emotional influences. 

One of the most striking results of our survey is that: 88% people feel that their teams have bias when making decision and roughly 65% feel that relevant data is available only sometimes or rarely.  

Other pain points and needs highlighted frequently were:

  • 'difficulties in gathering data​'
  • 'preference for recommendations tailored to our interests'
  • 'time taken in collaboration between people'
  • '6-8 weeks for decision preparation based on topic and impact​'
  • 'information is often distributed in different places like OneDrive, .pdfs, .docs, excels'
  • 'disconnect of decision need with data often results in missed opportunities and non-impactful decisions'

Qualitative Decision-Making (QDM) Innovation Project :

The Qualitative Decision-Making (QDM) project at SAP Research and Innovation center aims to transform how enterprises make decisions. Designed to augment the work of analysts, QDM empowers employees across functions—from finance to sustainability, production, and procurement—to make informed, researched, data-driven decisions quickly and efficiently. 

  1. We use Generative AI that provides us research, real time information sources, benchmarks, ideas, and analysis of data with reduced emotional or heuristic interference.  AI has strived to provide more impartial recommendations, guide teams toward alternative viewpoints, and even pause decision-making processes to ensure reflection. 
  2. Combine this with company's data in SAP looking over past history, future plans and processes/application/user information to bring decision options that are highly tailored and exclusive to you for a specific decision need.
  3. Final "cherry on top" - we determine the relevant key drivers for each AI generated, company specific decision strategy to simulate the effect of it in real time. 

For example, a Controller and a Supply Chain Specialist can perform risk analysis to mitigate risk of Tarifs imposed on some of their most selling products in the US. Demo of the this was shown by SAP leaders in latest Global Sapphire 2025 Keynote sessions in Orlando: 

And in this example then, for one of the generated mitigation strategy to shift some of the trade in other locations; exact key drivers from the SAP dataset (e.g.;  via SAC model) are identified with default input values that will lead to btter outcome: 

Disclaimer: Features and screens shown here are work in progress by SAP, subject to change and not guaranteed to be part of SAP product portfolio.

Other examples where QDM can be of a great help:

  • Use-case 1: Competitive analysis to consider new strategies for winning against competition.
  • Use-case 2: Pros and Cons of customers credit limits based on data from SAP e.g., account receivable and various credit agency ratings.
  • Use-case 3: Optimize decision making process for selecting manufacturing locations.
  • Use-case 4: Evaluate the most profitable scenario between in-house manufacturing and outsourcing, focused on cost-effectiveness.
  • Use-case 6: Compare options via Pro-Cons analysis to improved sustainability goals such as renewable content based on newly imposed regulatory guidelines.

Closing Remarks:

AI is evolving fast — and it's entering the realm of strategic decision-making. As HBR also showed, it can generate complex business ideas and presentations in 1/100th the time of human teams for decision making.

But the key question remains:
“Can I trust the results — or is it just hallucinating?”

That’s where we come in. By combining your internal SAP data with external unstructured sources using a RAG-based approach, and running LLMs with dynamic prompt engineering securely within your SAP environment, we ensure outcomes are relevant, high-quality, and trustworthy.

The result? Smarter decisions in seconds, powered by your own business logic in SAP.

entry.title: SAP BDC Key Features
entry.content:
SAP BDC Key Features

In my last blog post (SAP BDC for Dummies - SAP Community) I have explained the concept of SAP BDC .

Now let us see the key features of SAP BDC.

There are 5 key features of SAP BDC.

  1. BDC Cockpit
  2. Intelligent apps
  3. Knowledge Graphs
  4. Data Products
  5. Object store

 

  1. BDC Cockpit

The BDC Cockpit in SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is the central user interface for managing and orchestrating all components of the BDC environment. It plays a crucial role for administrators, data scientists, and analysts by providing a unified workspace to explore, configure, and deploy data-driven solutions.

The SAP Business Data Cloud Cockpit is:

  • The main entry point for interacting with the BDC platform.
  • A centralized UI for managing the data landscape, applications, and data products.
  • A tool for exploring, deploying, and activating intelligent applications and data packages.

Key Features of the BDC Cockpit

  • Landscape Configuration
    • Set up and manage connections to SAP and non-SAP source and target systems.
    • Define which components (e.g., SAP S/4HANA, SuccessFactors) are part of your SAP BDC data landscape.
  • Artifact Management
    • Data Packages: Bundled data models and dashboards for specific scenarios.
    • Intelligent Applications: Prebuilt apps for reporting and analytics.
    • Discover and deploy: View metadata like category, system type, version, and purpose.
  • Data Product Activation
    • Activate data packages to make Data Products available via Foundation Services.
    • These products can then be used in SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud, or AI/ML workflows.
  • User Roles
    • Administrators: Configure systems and manage access.
    • Data Scientists/Analysts: Deploy models, explore data, and build insights.

 2. Intelligent apps

 Intelligent Applications in SAP Business Data Cloud are AI-powered, adaptive applications designed to transform business operations by learning from data, understanding business context, and automating decision-making processes. 

 Each intelligent application in SAP Business Data Cloud is underpinned by curated, governed data products, sets of structured business data and metadata designed to accelerate use cases and reduce data integration overhead. And with SAP Databricks natively available in SAP Business Data Cloud, users can leverage SAP data products with existing lakehouses with a zero-copy approach.

An SAP Business Data Cloud administrator can install intelligent apps to the SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud tenants in the SAP Business Data Cloud formation.

When an intelligent app is installed:

  • SAP-managed spaces are created in SAP Datasphere to contain the insight app content.
  • Replication flows, tables, views, and analytic models are created in these spaces to ingest, prepare and expose the required data to SAP Analytics Cloud.

 3. Knowledge Graphs

SAP Business Data Cloud Knowledge Graphs refer to a powerful capability within SAP HANA Cloud and the broader SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) that enables businesses to connect, contextualize, and analyze their enterprise data using graph-based models. 

SAP Knowledge Graphs are semantic data structures that:

  • Connect structured and unstructured data across SAP systems.
  • Model relationships between business entities (e.g., customers, products, invoices).
  • Enable AI and analytics to understand business context more deeply.
  • Support natural language queries and intelligent automation.

They are now a general availability (GA) feature in SAP HANA Cloud as of Q1 2025(What’s New in SAP HANA Cloud – March 2025 - SAP Community)

Key Features of Knowledge Graphs 

  • Ontology-Driven Modelling
    • At its core, an ontology is a formal representation of knowledge within a particular domain. It defines the types of entities that exist in that domain and the relationships between them.

                   Example: A "Vendor" might be linked to "Orders", "Invoices", and "Support Tickets".

    • Knowledge graphs use ontologies to define business concepts and relationships.
  • Multi-Model Integration
    • Works alongside relational, spatial, JSON, and vector engines in SAP HANA Cloud.
    • Enables a data fabric approach—unifying diverse data types and formats.
  • AI Enablement
    • Powers SAP Joule and other AI agents by grounding them in real business data.
    • Reduces hallucinations in AI outputs by providing context-aware answers 
  • Natural Language Access
    • Users can query data using plain language.
    • AI agents can retrieve and act on data across SAP systems.
  • Pre-Populated Graphs
    • Comes with pre-built knowledge graphs for common SAP modules (e.g., S/4HANA, SuccessFactors).

Read more about knowledge graphs in Connecting the Facts: SAP HANA Cloud’s Knowledge G... - SAP Community

4. Data Products

Data Product is a grouping concept that carries the relevant metadata to describe the dataset and its data qualities. A Data Product itself is not directly consumable, but it exposes data through output ports, which can be implemented as APIs or events. In the first iteration of BDC, the primary Data Products expose a single output port as delta-sharing API. In principle, there can be multiple output ports of various API and/or event types. To understand data ingestion, lineage, and dependencies, Data Products can also have input ports which are the equivalent of an ORD Integration Dependency that lists the APIs and events that are used to ingest or process the data.

Types of Data Products: There are three main types of Data Products:

  • SAP-managed Data Products: Pre-built by SAP, focusing on data from ERP and line-of-business applications.
  • Customer-defined Data Products: Created by customers to meet specific analytical needs, leveraging data from various sources.
  • Partner-defined Data Products: Developed by SAP partners, offering specialized datasets tailored to specific industries or use cases. 

5. Object store

The Object Store in SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC)—delivered via the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)—is a cloud-native storage service designed to handle unstructured data such as files, documents, images, and large binary objects (BLOBs).

This service is particularly useful for handling large amounts of unstructured data, such as documents, images, and backups.

Key Features of Object Store

  1. Instance Sharing: The Object Store on SAP BTP supports instance sharing, which allows multiple applications to share databases, messaging queues, and other services. This feature helps in reusing computing resources, exchanging data, and reducing costs.
  2. Integration with Hyperscalers: The Object Store can be integrated with various hyperscaler options such as Azure Blob Storage, Amazon S3, and Google Cloud Storage. This flexibility allows users to leverage their existing cloud infrastructure.
  3. Service Plans: The Object Store offers different service plans, with the 'standard' plan enabling the instance sharing functionality. Users need to upgrade to the 'standard' plan to utilize this feature.

 

These are key features in SAP BDC for now.

 

Happy Learning!

 

Swati Gawade-Shingade.

Cognizant technology Solutions.

entry.title: CDS: How to use @AnalyticsDetails.elimination annotation to eliminate the internal business volume
entry.content:
This blog is about how to use @AnalyticsDetails.elimination to eliminate the internal business volume when executing an analytical query.

Internal business volume

When revenues made between two subsidiaries of company, for company’s total revenue, these revenues should not be considered, as transaction is happening within the company. For example, a company is divided into several subsidiaries. These subsidiaries are divided into further subsidiaries. This means there exists a hierarchy of subsidiaries. Subsidiaries are selling products to each other. For each subsidiary this is external sales. But for a parent subsidiary this is just internal sales, as both subsidiaries are descendants. By using this annotation, revenues made between two subsidiaries in an organization are no longer displayed.

More information about the concept can be found Elimination of Internal Business Volume | SAP Help Portal

Syntax

elimination: {

     pair : array of {

       dimension1: String(30);

       dimension2: String(30);

     }

     rule : String(20) enum { ELIMINATE_IF_ALL; ELIMINATE_IF_ONE; };

   }

The annotation can be used in cube-views (Analytics.dataCategory: #CUBE) or in analytical queries (define transient view entity with provider contract analytical query).

  • pair: this list defines the pairs (dimension1 and dimension2) which are checked for elimination. Both dimensions have needed a foreign key association to the same target view. If the annotation is used on cube level, both dimensions must be elements of the cube. If the annotation is used in an analytical query, both dimensions must be part of the source cube view.
  • rule: if set to #ELIMINATE_IF_ALL, then the volume is eliminated if all pairs are internal. If set to #ELIMINATE_IF_ONE the volume is eliminated if at least one pair is internal. If "rule" is not set, the AND-logic (ELIMINATE_IF_ALL) is applied.

Example 

Following example displays the number of seat booked in flight according to the elimination rule specified on destination airport, departure airport, destination country, departure country.

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_ALLOWED
@EndUserText.label: 'Elimination of Internal Busniess volumn'
@ObjectModel.modelingPattern: #ANALYTICAL_QUERY
@ObjectModel.supportedCapabilities: [#ANALYTICAL_QUERY]
define transient view entity ZLR_PC_FLIGHTICE_SIMPLE
  provider contract analytical_query
  as projection on ZOQ_FLIGHT_ICE
{

  _airportfrom._hier( p_HierarchyID : 'GEO' ) as _airportFromHier,
  @Consumption.hidden: true
  _airportfrom.AirportID                      as dummyAirportFrom,


  _airportto._hier( p_HierarchyID : 'GEO' )   as _airportToHier,
  @Consumption.hidden: true
  _airportto.AirportID                        as dummyAirportTo,
  
  @AnalyticsDetails.query: {
    axis: #ROWS,
    displayHierarchy: #ON,
    hierarchyAssociation: '_airportFromHier'
  }
  
  airportfrom,
  
  @AnalyticsDetails.query: {
    axis: #ROWS,
    displayHierarchy: #ON,
    hierarchyAssociation: '_airportToHier'
  }
  airportto,

  @Consumption.filter.defaultValue: 'DE'
  @AnalyticsDetails.query.axis: #ROWS
  countryfrom,

  @AnalyticsDetails.query.axis: #ROWS
  countryto,

  seatsmax,



  @AnalyticsDetails.elimination: {
    pair: [{ dimension1 : 'countryfrom' , dimension2: 'countryto' }]
  }
  @EndUserText.label: 'SeatsMax Q-ICE country'
  seatsmax                                    as seatsmaxICEQ_C,


  @AnalyticsDetails.elimination:{
    pair: [ { dimension1 : 'airportfrom' , dimension2: 'airportto' },
            { dimension1 : 'countryfrom' , dimension2: 'countryto' } ],
    rule: #ELIMINATE_IF_ALL
  }
  @EndUserText.label: 'SeatsMax Q-ICE airport and country'
  seatsmax                                    as seatsmaxICEQ_AC,


  @AnalyticsDetails.elimination:{
    pair: [ { dimension1 : 'airportfrom' , dimension2: 'airportto' },
            { dimension1 : 'countryfrom' , dimension2: 'countryto' } ],
    rule: #ELIMINATE_IF_ONE
  }
  @EndUserText.label: 'SeatsMax Q-ICE airport or country'
  seatsmax                                    as seatsmaxICEQ_A_C


}

 

Output

 

In this example for columns (cells marked with colour): 

SeatsMax Q-ICE country – elimination pair mentioned as 'countryfrom' and 'countryto'. Therefore, there is no entry where departure country and destination country are same.

SeatsMax Q-ICE airport and country- elimination pairs are 'airportfrom' and 'airportto', 'countryfrom' and 'countryto' And rule as #ELIMINATE_IF_ALL, Therefore, there is no entry where destination airport and departure airport are same AND destination country and departure country are same.

SeatsMax Q-ICE airport or country - elimination pairs are 'airportfrom' and 'airportto', 'countryfrom' and 'countryto' And rule as #ELIMINATE_IF_ONE, Therefore, there is no entry where destination airport and departure airport are same OR destination country and departure country are same.

 

Constraints

The annotation can be used in cubes or in queries.

  • The dimension names in one pair (AnalyticsDetails.elimination.pair) must have a foreign key association to the same dimension view and this dimension view must have an hierarchy association.
  • In the query definition:
    • It is not necessary that any of the dimensions in the list of pairs is part of the query
    • If a hierarchy is assigned to a dimension, then the pair dimension must be assigned to the same hierarchy or must be without hierarchy
    • If a dimension is not in drill down, then the condition of the one pair is always true (independent of the value of the other dimension of the pair). The same is true on totals/subtotal lines.

entry.title: Sunset of BEx Tools - Frequently Asked Questions and Answers (FAQ)
entry.content:
Sunset of BEx Tools - Frequently Asked Questions and Answers (FAQs):

With support for BEx Analyzer, Workbooks, and Broadcaster ending in October 2025, organizations using SAP BW need to act now. This article answers the most frequently asked questions around the BEx tool sunset, outlines key dates, risks, and support limitations, and explores recommended replacement tools like SAP Analysis for Office and SAP Analytics Cloud

We have grouped the questions by components to make the information clear, simple, and component-specific.

  • General Questions about End of Support
  • Migration and Replacement Tools
  • Technical and Operational Concerns
  • Conversion Questions
  • License
  • Planning and Next Steps

For an Overview of the BEx tools sunset including the rationale, timelines, and key actions customers should consider ahead of October 2025, refer to previous detailed blog post:
👉Sunset of BEx Tools in SAP BW – What You Need to Know Before October 2025 This foundational article complements the FAQs by offering strategic guidance for planning your transition effectively.

 

General Questions about End of Support:

What specific BEx tools are affected by the end of support in October 2025?

  • BEx tools dependent on excel, such as BEx Analyzer, BEx Workbooks, and BEx Broadcaster (Pre-calculation server) are impacted

What are the key dates we need to be aware of for the end of support?

  • October 14, 2025: End of Microsoft support for Office 2016 and 2019, impacting BEx Analyzer / Workbook and BEx Broadcaster (Pre-calculation server)
  • December 31, 2027: End of mainstream maintenance for SAP BW 7.5.
  • December 31, 2030: End of extended maintenance for SAP BW 7.5 and BI Java based Broadcasting and BEx Query Designer
  • Note: Users on older SAP BW versions (e.g., 7.4 or earlier) have already past their mainstream maintenance end dates in 2020.

What happens if we continue to use BEx Excel dependent tools after the end of support dates?

  • You may face several risks, including:
    • No SAP Support: SAP will no longer provide patches, bug fixes, or technical assistance for issues related to impacted BEx tools. Incident support will not be available in case of any issues faced.
    • Compatibility Problems: Future updates to operating systems, Office versions, SAP GUI Patches etc. may break BEx functionality.
    • Security and Compliance Risks: Unsupported software is more vulnerable to security threats. Without security updates from Microsoft or SAP, any vulnerabilities discovered after the end-of-support date will remain unpatched, increasing the risk of data breaches or compliance violations
    • No Future Enhancements: There will be no new features or improvements for BEx Excel-dependent tools. Any issues with integration, performance, or usability will need to be managed internally, without help from SAP

Is there any way to get support for BEx Excel dependent tools after the October 14’ 2025?

  • No. There is no Extended maintenance or Customer specific maintenance available for BEx Analyzer and BEx Broadcaster support beyond October 2025, even at additional cost.

What about embedded BW systems (e.g., ECC, SCM, CRM) using BEx tools, are they also impacted?

  • Yes. Any system with the SAP_BW component, including embedded BW in SAP ERP systems, is impacted if excel based BEx tools are used for reporting.

Migration and Replacement Tools:

What are the recommended replacement tools for BEx Analyzer and BEx Workbooks?

  • SAP recommends transitioning to:
    • SAP Analysis for Microsoft Office (AO) for Excel-based reporting with similar layout and filtering capabilities. SAP offers Mass Conversion Tool through SAP Services to convert existing BEx Workbooks to Analysis for Office workbooks. Manual conversion of BEx to AO workbook also possible.
    • SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Excel Add-in for modern, web-based dashboards, stories, and advanced analytics. There is No automated conversion tool exist for converting BEx Workbooks directly to SAC Excel Add-in. Migration requires manual rebuilding of reports and dashboards in SAC

What are the recommended replacement tools for BEx Broadcasting (Pre-calculation server)?

  • Broadcasting / Scheduling in SAP BW systems is used to schedule and distribute BEx Query and Workbook outputs via email, portal, or printer, leveraging different SAP broadcasting solutions such as Pre-calculation Server, BI Java Scheduling, SAC Publication, Analysis for office scheduling
  • BEx Broadcaster (Pre-calculation Server) support will end in October 2025, aligning with the broader SAP BEx sunset
  • Broadcasting via BI Java scheduling is available but not considered a long-term strategic option due to planned EOMM by 2027 and EOEM by 2030
  • Recommended Alternatives for scheduling and distribution:
    • SAP BusinessObjects Business Intelligence: Offers built-in scheduling capabilities through the SAP BusinessObjects platform, including the ability to schedule Analysis for Office Workbooks
    • SAP Analytics Cloud Publication: Strategic SAP solution for modern, cloud-based report distribution and scheduling

What are the recommended replacement tools for BEx Web Application Designer (WAD)?

  • BEx Web Application Designer is supported with BW on HANA and BW/4HANA until 2030, subject to the end of mainstream maintenance in 2027 and the availability of extended maintenance through 2030.
  • SAP recommends replacing BEx Web with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) as primary replacement tool

Is there any impact on BI Java or BOBJ based Broadcasting tools with this BEx tool sunset?

  • No. there is no impact to BI Java or BOBJ based broadcasting tools.
  • However, BI Java based broadcasting is supported as per the maintenance timelines for BW 7.50 i.e. until 2027 (mainstream maintenance) and until 2030 (Extended Maintenance) with BW 7.5 and BW/4HANA.

What are the migration paths for our existing BEx workbooks to Analysis for Office?

  • For customers with a large number of BEx Workbooks, we recommend engaging with SAP MaxAttention or the SAP Services team, which offers a mass conversion tool to automate the migration of BEx Workbooks to Analysis for Office format
  • For customers with only a limited number of BEx Workbooks, a manual migration  using Analysis for Office may be more practical

What are the migration paths for our existing BEx workbooks to SAC?

  • There is currently no automated conversion tool available to migrate BEx Workbooks directly to SAC
  • As a result, any migration from BEx Workbooks to SAC requires a manual rebuild of the report content using SAC’s capabilities

What are the migration paths for our existing BEx Broadcasting to SAC Publication?

  • There is no direct migration path or automated tool to convert existing BEx Broadcaster (Pre-calculation server) to SAC Publications
  • Customers must manually recreate broadcasting logic using SAC’s Publication and Scheduling features

What resources or support does SAP offer for this migration?

  • For customers with a large volume of BEx Workbooks, SAP MaxAttention or SAP Services team provide:
    • A mass conversion tool developed internally by SAP Services to automate workbook migration
    • Expert-led migration assessments, scoping, and planning workshops
    • Support for functional validation and post-migration support

Technical and Operational Concerns:

Will our existing SAP BW system (e.g., BW 7.5) still be compatible with BEx tools if we don't upgrade Office?

  • Technically, your SAP BW 7.5 system will continue to support the excel independent BEx tools (BEx Query Designer) until the end of its own maintenance (mainstream until December 31, 2027, and extended until 2030).
  • However. excel dependent tools (BEx Analyzer/Workbooks, BEx Broadcasting – Pre-calculation server) will have end of support by October 2025.

If we are planning to move to SAP BW/4HANA, what is the role of BEx tools in that landscape?

  • BEx tools are not supported with BW/4HANA and already has replacement path
    • BEx Analyzer / Workbooks à Analysis for Office (Conversion)
    • BEx Broadcasting (Pre-calculation server) àBI Java Broadcasting / BOBJ scheduling / SAC Publication (Rebuild)
    • BEx Queries à Queries (No changes)
    • BEx Query Designer à Eclipsed based BW-MT Modeling tool (Modeling tool change)

If customers continue using BEx Analyzer / Workbooks / BEx Broadcaster with Office 365 or newer platforms, and something breaks, will SAP support incidents still be accepted?

  • No. If issues arise due to platform incompatibility, SAP support incidents will not be accepted or resolved for these legacy tools.
  • Customers are strongly encouraged to migrate to SAP Analysis for Office and SAP Analytics Cloud, which are actively supported and compatible with modern office environments.

Since BEx Analyzer is tightly coupled with SAP GUI for Windows, will SAP continue to deliver SAP GUI updates (compatible with BEx) for the full NetWeaver 7.5 maintenance window (i.e., through end of 2027 or 2030 extended)?

  • No GUI updates will be delivered related to obsolete BEx tools (BEx Analyzer / Workbooks and BEx Broadcasting (Pre-calculation server)

Are there any planned deprecations or changes in SAP GUI that could break BEx Analyzer compatibility before NetWeaver 7.5 is retired?

  • Yes, there are planned deprecations and technical shifts in SAP GUI and the Microsoft Office ecosystem that could affect BEx Analyzer compatibility, even before SAP NetWeaver 7.5 reaches end of mainstream maintenance in December 2027

Will our existing BEx Queries still work with the BEx Query designer tools?

  • You can still design and maintain queries using BEx Query Designer until the end of 2030
  • If you are running BW 7.5 on HANA or BW/4HANA, you also have the option to use the Eclipse-based BW Modeling Tools (BW-MT) for query design and modeling

Will my existing BEx Workbooks continue to work after Office 2016/2019 support ends?

  • Technically, BEx Workbooks may work after October 14’ 2025, however there will no SAP support for issues
  • Because Microsoft and SAP will stop providing security updates, any new vulnerabilities found after this date will remain unpatched, increasing the risk of security breaches
  • BEx tools are delivered as Frontend Patches (BEx FEPs) built specifically for Office 2016/2019, including signed files (.xla, .exe). After support ends, SAP will no longer release these installation files for Excel-dependent BEx tools. This means you can only keep using BEx Analyzer and Workbooks if you stay on a previously released BEx FEP version and keep Excel 2016 or 2019 installed. Even then, SAP support will be very limited, only addressing known issues fixed in earlier patches or backend problems, with no new bug fixes for the frontend
  • In addition, there is a potential risk that Microsoft’s compatibility between Office 365 and Office 2016 or 2019 might break. If this happens, SAP will not be able to provide any support for Office 365 in relation to BEx tools

Are there any plans to support BEx Analyzer and BEx workbooks beyond October 2025, especially for customers planning their BW strategy over the next several years with SAP BW 7.5 PCE?

  • There is No long-term support of BEx Analyzer, Workbooks and BEx Broadcasting (Pre-calculation). SAP can only support such versions of third-party products (MS Office) for which the third-party vendor (Microsoft) still provides maintenance, and in the case of excel dependent BEx tools, the last supported Office versions are Office 2016 and Office 2019.
  • No support offered beyond October 14, 2025 for Excel dependent BEx tools.

Will SAP continue to release SAP GUI updates (compatible with BEx Analyzer) for the full NetWeaver 7.5 maintenance window (through end of 2027 or 2030 extended)?

  • SAP NetWeaver 7.5 will be supported until the end of 2027, with optional extended maintenance until the end of 2030.
  • SAP will continue to ship the BEx FEPs that are compatible with SAP GUI, but these will only contain the BEx tools that do not have a dependency with MS Office. i.e., after October 14th, these will no longer contain the installation files for BEx Analyzer and BEx Broadcasting (Pre-calculation). This means, installing the new FEP will remove any existing installation of the excel based tools.
  • While it may still be technically possible to use BEx Analyzer and Pre-calculation server if the old installation files were preserved, this will no longer qualify as a standard installation of BEx Tools and SAP cannot guarantee that it will work without issues.

Conversion Questions:

When we convert a report from BEx to AFO, does the output stay in the same format (ie same data in same columns)?

  • Mostly Yes, the table structure and measure/dimensions are replicated like in the source system, which includes respecting common filtering conditions.
    • Underlying query logic is preserved when opening BEx workbooks in AFO.
    • However, UI rendering, formatting, and charting might look different as AFO uses Excel's engine, so custom formatting from BEx might need manual tweaks.

Is there any limitation on the data AFO can access/any reason to think that if we have something in a BEx report we can’t get it in an AFO report?

  • No, additional BW authorizations should be required to refresh a BEx Query in Analysis for Office compared to BEx Analyzer.

How do users access AFO? Does it depend/matter which SAP GUI users are on?

  • Analysis for Office is a client tool which will need to be installed on the end user’s machines. There is no dependency on SAP GUI. Refer to https://support.sap.com/content/dam/launchpad/en_us/pam/pam-essentials/AO_28_PAM_Additional_Info.pdf for more details

What are the limitations of conversion of BEx to AfO Workbooks using Mass Conversion tool?

  • The following features are not automatically converted but required some adjustment: Styles​, Conditions, Exceptions​, VBA API​, Navigation Panes, Dropdown Boxes, Button​s
  • The workbooks with given features can be identified during the Pre-Assessment

How can we ensure functional parity when replacing BEx queries with modern tools?

  • SAP reporting tools such as Analysis for Office, Business Objects Business Intelligence, and SAP Analytics Cloud do not replace the need for BEx Queries. Instead, BEx Queries are consumed by these solutions to query the data.

For conversion effort, let say few thousand of server workbooks were saved in the BW. Is there a way to assess which are effectively in use?

  • Yes, Pre-assessment service from SAP will identify the Workbooks (saved on BW server) which are frequently used. Local workbooks saved on user desktop can not be identified as part of this assessment.

License:

What are the licensing implications when moving from BEx to new tools like SAP Analysis for Office or SAP Analytics Cloud?

  • SAP Analysis for Office and SAP Analytics Cloud required additional licensing which is not provided with an SAP BW license. Please discuss the options with your Account Executive.

Is license for Analysis for office based on number of users?

  • Analysis for Office, Standard Edition is available to use if you have a BusinessObjects license.
  • Analysis for Office, SAC Edition is available if you have an SAC subscription. The SAC Edition does require you to have an SAC user, so it is effectively user-based licensing.

Do customer need to buy additional license if they already have BOBJ license?

  • No, every BOBJ license also includes Analysis for Office.

Planning and Next Steps:

What are the first steps we should take to prepare for the BEx end of support?

  • Assess your current BEx landscape: Identify all BEx reports, their usage, and dependencies.
  • Educate stakeholders: Inform relevant business and IT teams about the upcoming changes and their implications.
  • Evaluate replacement tools: Understand the features and capabilities of SAP Analysis for Microsoft Office and SAP Analytics Cloud.
  • Develop a migration plan: Outline timelines, resources, and a phased approach if necessary.

Who should be involved in planning and executing the migration from BEx?

  • A cross-functional team is ideal, including IT (SAP BW/BI specialists), business users from various departments who rely on BEx reports, and may be project management.

What kind of training will our users and developers need for the new tools?

  • Users will need training on how to use SAP Analysis for Microsoft Office or SAP Analytics Cloud for report consumption, ad-hoc analysis, and potentially report creation.
  • Developers will need training on the new development environments and best practices for the chosen replacement tools

Conclusion:

We have made every effort to cover the important FAQs, however we understand you may have additional questions. Please refer to standard SAP guides or Feel free to use the comments section to ask them. We are committed to keeping this blog up-to-date with the latest information. We hope this has been a helpful resource.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Pong Game - A Fun Fusion of Custom Widgets and Standard Functionalities
entry.content:
Introduction

Have you ever wondered what happens when the worlds of gaming and business analytics collide? Well, today, I am about to take you on an exciting journey where we’ll explore just that. In this blog, I’ll demonstrate a Pong game in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) which combines the power of custom widgets and standard functionalities.

Are you ready to spice up your analytics environment? Let’s dive into it!

 

Why Build a Pong Game in SAP Analytics Cloud?

You might be wondering, 'Why a Pong Game?'. Pong is a classic game, known for its simple mechanics and fun appeal. Furthermore, think about the broad insights you can gain by combining it with a powerful analytics platform like SAP Analytics Cloud. In the end, we want to embed the Pong Game into a SAC story, allowing users to play the game and then review simple statistics. This dynamic interaction should showcase the wide-ranging possibilities with custom widgets and create an engaging experience that might be perfect for some meetings, team-building activities, sales demos, or just making your analytics environment more interactive and fun.

Within this project, we can explore:

  • Custom Widgets:
    Extends SAP Analytics Cloud beyond its standard capabilities by embedding custom visualizations and interactive elements.

  • Planning/Reporting:
    Use SAP’s built-in functionalities to store and track your gaming stats

 

Architecture

 

Building the Pong Game

To play the Pong game within SAP Analytics Cloud, you’ll need to develop a custom widget using at least two components:

  1. JavaScript File for Game Logic (Main.js):
    Write a script to control the movement of the ball and the paddles, handle collision detection and much more. This code will define the game's behavior and interactivity.



  2. JSON File (Index.json):
    This file specifies the custom widget, defining details such as its name, description, and version. It also allows you to define properties, methods, and events, which store game-related data at runtime like player scores or ball speed. You can reference this JSON file in your JavaScript to adjust game settings dynamically based on user interaction.

With these components, you'll be able to create the core functionality of the game. I used Visual Studio Code as development environment but feel free to use a code editor of your choice. In the end you will just upload the mentioned files in SAC, where they’ll be hosted.

 

Storing Custom Widget Game Results in SAC

Unfortunately, there is currently no possibility to directly write data with custom widgets into SAC models. Although there are open APIs like the PUSH based Data Import Service which allows you to import fact and master data to SAP Analytics Cloud, I chose a simpler approach to avoid unnecessary complexity and efforts required to set up such integrations.

Let’s combine the custom widget with SAC’s standard functionalities.

Prerequisites

  1. Custom Widget Properties: 
    Define properties in the custom widget to hold and update the data dynamically, such as the scores for Player 1 and Player 2. I set the default value to -1 so you can easily identify if a score wasn’t set correctly.

     







  2. Custom Widget Methods: Next, we define a getter method that allow us to retrieve the values of these properties within SAC.






  3. Model & Data Action: Now we just need a model in the SAC to store the game-related data from the widget and set up a data action to push the data into the model. We’ll use a basic model with relevant dimensions and measures to store game data.



  4. SAC Story (Optimized Design Experience)

 

Workflow overview

There are two main game modes:

  • Single Player: Play against a basic, rule-based AI bot (no fancy learning algorithms here, just a classic bot minding its own business).

  • Multiplayer: Play against a colleague on the same keyboard using simple controls like ‘W’ and ‘S’ for Player 1 and the ‘Up’ and ‘Down’ arrow keys for Player 2.

While it would have been possible to manage game IDs and players entirely within the custom widget, I chose to use standard elements to reduce widget complexity and test the integration capability. In the end, both ways lead to sufficient results.

But enough talk - A picture is worth a thousand words. A GIF even more. Let's illustrate the workflow with a practical example of the implementation:

Single Player

Multi Player

As you can see, the game flow is simple. We create players and a unique game ID. Afterwards, the game is played, and the results are stored via data action. In addition, I added a hierarchy to the game dimension, enabling us to group matches under parent nodes. This helps to distinguish between different game sessions - perfect for scenarios like playing a best-of-three series against a colleague.

Let’s quickly clarify, how the creation of transactional data works.
Like already mentioned, we dynamically update the custom widgets properties to store the goals each player achieves and derive which player won / lost the game. These properties can be read using custom methods we define in our custom widget like ‘getScore()’. Adding ‘console.log()’, we can display the game result in the console.

Now, we can use these values to set numeric parameters of a data action to persist the results in our model.

 

Reporting

Once the game is played and data is stored, you can easily build a report to visualize your stats. Of course, the possibilities are endless - this could be expanded to track win rates, visualizing seasons and tournaments or even predict game outcomes. But for now, we’ve kept it simple.

 

Extras

Alongside the core gameplay, I added a few extra touches with the custom widget:

  • Custom Themes: A custom theme can change the ball to a logo and change the color of different parts like the paddles.

  • Game Music: A theme melody plays in the background during the game, with sound effects for paddle collisions, a victory sound when you win, and a sad tone for losing.

  • Practice Mode: A mode that allows players to practice without storing any data.

 

Key Insights to Take Away:

  1. Custom Widgets Are Cool: The ability to create interactive, personalized widgets within SAP Analytics Cloud is an incredible feature. Custom widgets enable you to go beyond standard functionalities, like embedding games for a bit of fun.

  2. Bring Planning to Life: The integration of planning functionality with a game, like tracking your scores, demonstrates how SAC can blend analytics with custom interactive experiences.

  3. Creative Opportunities: The combination of SAC's customization and standard features allows for many use cases. Whether you are creating a game or a more complex business-related scenario, the toolset allows creativity.

Ready to give custom widgets a try? Dive in, and let the games begin! 😊 🎮

Cheers,

Tim

entry.title: Pass Data Action Parameter Value Directly from Dropdowns through Script in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)
entry.content:
SAP Analytics Cloud allows you to create interactive dashboards and automate actions using scripting. A common requirement is to pass values selected in dropdowns directly into a Data Action. This guide walks through how to link dropdown inputs to Data Action parameters using scripting.

Step 1: Create the Data Action

  • Go to the Main Menu and select Data Action.
  • Create a new Data Action.
  • Define your dynamic parameters.
  • Add your required calculations or steps using those parameters.

Example: Let’s assume your Data Action uses 1 measure and 1 dimension.

Step 2: Set Up the Story

  • Open your story from the menu bar or create a new one.
  • Insert a Table and two Dropdowns.
    (Refer to our earlier blog if you need help creating dropdowns. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/interlinked-dropdown-filters-using-script-in-sap-analytics-cloud-sac-story/ba-p/14103275 )

Step 3: Set Up the Data Action in the Story

  1. In the left-side panel, open the Scripting section.
  2. Click the + and choose Data Action.
  3. Name your data action (e.g., DataAction_1).

     4. In the Detail section, assign the data action you created earlier.

Step 4: Add a Trigger Button

  1. Go to the Insert menu and add a Button to your story.
  2. Select the Button, then open the Script Editor.
  3. In the onClick() event, use the following script as an example:
var pVersion=Dropdown_1.getSelectedKey();
var pMeasure=Dropdown_2.getSelectedKey();

DataAction_1.setParameterValue("TargetVersion",pVersion);
DataAction_1.setParameterValue("measure1",pMeasure);

if(DataAction_1.execute()){
Application.showMessage(ApplicationMessageType.Success,"DA run succesfully");
Application.refreshData();}
  • Here, Targetversion and measure1 are the parameter names defined in your Data Action.
  • Dropdown_1 and Dropdown_2 are the IDs of your dropdown widgets.

Step 5: View Mode and Execution

  • Switch to View Mode.
  • Use the dropdowns to filter or select values.
  • Click the Button.
  • The Data Action will execute with the parameter values fetched directly from the dropdowns.

Conclusion

By using scripting in SAC stories, you can efficiently pass dropdown values into Data Actions. This approach makes your dashboards dynamic and user-driven, enabling better interactivity and automation.

This pattern is ideal for what-if scenarios, scenario planning, or on-the-fly calculations based on user inputs.

Try adapting it to your own use cases and streamline the planning experience!

entry.title: Release Navigator for SAP BTP - What's New in Q2 2025
entry.content:
The Release Navigator for SAP BTP continues to evolve and expand, offering our customers improved resources and streamlined access to product updates. As we step into Q2 2025, we are excited to announce the addition of 11 new product pages. Read more about the details in this blog post.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Collaborating closely with SAP BTP experts, we've expanded the release coverage by introducing 11 new product pages. These pages compile links to all available release information, such as schedules, pre- and post-release communications, blogs, and more:

  • SAP Audit Log Service
  • SAP Authorization and Trust Management Service
  • SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry environment
  • SAP Connectivity Service
  • SAP Credential Store
  • SAP Custom Domain Service
  • SAP Destination Service
  • SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh
  • SAP Malware Scanning Service
  • SAP Private Link Service
  • SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI

You will notice that several of these pages share certain resources which are relevant across SAP BTP services that are relating to security.

Here's a glimpse at our new additions:

This page contains specific resources for SAP Audit Log service as well as key release resources for SAP BTP security services, which also partially cover SAP Audit Log service among others. Here’s a list of what you can find on this page:

More pages dedicated to specific products and services are expected in future updates.

 

For centralized information regarding the release of new features for SAP BTP products and services, consult the Consolidated Release Schedule for SAP BTP. Regarding the Release Navigator updates, refer to this update plan:

*Disclaimer: The schedule is subject to alterations without notice, including potential delays or the integration of changes in subsequent update windows. It doesn't establish a commitment, promise, or legal duty to deliver any material, code, or functionality. Looking into the remaining quarters of 2025, updates will focus on quarterly content maintenance and a steady extension of product coverage. No substantial UI updates are planned for the time being.

 

The Readiness@Scale team within SAP Enterprise Support is driven to ensure release readiness across the SAP Enterprise Support portfolio. Our mission is to empower SAP customers to effectively adopt and utilize innovations.

If you have feedback or specific requests for content within the release navigator, feel free to contact us.

Don't forget to explore our team’s blogs tagged under Readiness@Scale.

Happy navigating!

Readiness@Scale  
SAP Enterprise Support

entry.title: Introducing SAP Analytics cloud Widget API
entry.content:
Great News! With the latest releases of 2025.08 for the SAP Analytics Cloud fast track customers (BI weekly release model) and for Quarterly release cycle with 2025 Q2 QRC, SAP Analytics Cloud Widget API  is now Generally available.

 

What is SAP Analytics Cloud widget API?

The SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Widget API is a powerful tool that allows developers to embed charts from SAC stories into external applications. This blog will provide an overview of the Widget API, walk you through the integration process, and demonstrate how it can enhance your application’s analytical capabilities

Overview of the Widget API

The Widget API in SAP Analytics Cloud enables seamless embedding of SAC visualisations into external web applications, portals, or custom-built systems. With this API, you can:

  • Display interactive charts within web applications.
  • Control the content programmatically (filter, refresh, resize, etc.).
  • Ensure secure communication between your application and SAC via authentication.
  • Achieve better user experience by embedding analytics directly into Desktop and web applications.

Prerequisites

Before getting started, ensure that you have:

  • Access to SAP Analytics Cloud with the required permissions. Ex: User will be able to log in to SAP Analytics cloud and view access to stories.
  • A widgetID, storyID, TenatID that you want to embed. (Refer the section3 on how to get widget ID)
  • Basic knowledge of JavaScript and HTML.

Widget API Features

Some key capabilities of the Widget API include:

  • Embedding Charts: Integrate single or multiple charts from SAC stories.
  • Filtering and Parameters: Apply filters and programmatically to change the view.
  • Context Menu & Interactions: Charts will be interactive, and you can filter on data point selection and apply various interactive options.
  • Cross-Origin Communication: Secure communication between the parent application and SAC.

Step-by-Step Integration of SAC Charts Using Widget API

  1. Generate the Embed Link: 

In SAP Analytics Cloud:

  • Open the story in edit mode that you want to embed.
  • Navigate the widget that you want to embed and get . You’ll use this in your web application. (Refer the section3 on how to get widget ID)

      2. Set Up Your Web Application

       Here’s a simple HTML structure to integrate the SAC widget into a web page.

  3. Understanding the API Structure:

This API enables seamless embedding of widgets from SAP Analytics Cloud into external applications. It comprises three main elements: a namespace, a function call, and a set of parameters (some mandatory, some optional).

These are the elements of the API:

  • Name Space:

This defines the scope for accessing the API components. The designated namespace is:**sap.sac.api.widget**

  • Function Call:

The primary function to invoke is: **renderWidget**

  • Required Parameters:

To successfully render a widget, the following parameters must be provided:

The call is renderWidget.

  • The following parameters are mandatory:
  • Container ID
    Specifies the HTML container (such as a <div>) in your application where the widget should be embedded
  • Tenant URL
    The base URL of your SAP Analytics Cloud tenant where the widget resides.
    Example: https://sample.tenant.cloud/sap/fpa/ui
  • SAC Story ID

SAC story ID that contains the widget.

  • To retrieve the Story ID, open the story in SAC and extract it from the URL.
    Example URL: https://sample.tenant.cloud/sap/fpa/ui/app.html#/story2&/s2/C7D03902C194A72F5F130EC083762CCA

→ Story ID: C7D03902C194A72F5F130EC083762CCA

  • Widget ID

Indicates the specific widget you want to embed. In edit mode, you can find it in the Styling panel under Generic Properties.

 

In view mode, right-click on the widget, go to More Options > Copy Widget ID.

 For example, the widget ID could be chart_2.

Other Optional parameters

The following parameters are optional in the API:

  • Enable and disable the widget Interaction:

You can disable the widget interaction for the users by passing these optional parameters.

Note: By default, all these parameters are set it to true.

  • enableInteraction: true/false

With this attribute, you can enable or disable the interaction in the widget.

  • enableMenu: true/false

With this attribute, you can enable or disable the context menu for the widget.

  • showHeader: true/false

With this attribute, you can show or hide the header of the widget.

  • showFooter: true/false

With this attribute, you can show or hide the footer of the widget.

Example:

4. Authentication & Security Considerations

For secure communication, SAC requires authentication. Depending on your setup:

  • Use SAML-based SSO if your users are already authenticated in the external application.

Ensure your SAC instance and the external application are configured to allow Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) for seamless data exchange.

5.Use Case: Embedded Analytics in a Customer Portal

Imagine a scenario where a bank uses a customer portal to provide users with real-time insights into their spending patterns. By embedding SAC charts into the portal using the Widget API, the bank can:

  • Display interactive dashboards tailored to each user’s transactions.
  • Allow users to filter data by date or category directly from the portal.
  • Automatically refresh the charts when new transactions occur.

This integration enhances the user experience and keeps customers engaged with actionable insights.

Reference Application:

We have released SAP Analytics Cloud, add-in for Microsoft PowerPoint, by using SAC widget API and You can now embed the SAP Analytics Cloud story widgets into PowerPoint as an Image which can be dynamically updated with the latest Data.

7. Example: Sample webpage integrating SAC widget using widget API

  • Install Visual Studio Code

https://code.visualstudio.com/

  • Install the “Live Server” extension.

  • Open the sample html code in Visual studio code application!

  • Add SAC tenant details in the initialization displaywidget function.

  • Add the widget ID in the displaywidget function

 

  • Click the “Go Live” button to start the server and it will run the application in the browser.

  • Additionally, you can pass the filters and Variables:
  sap.sac.api.widget.renderWidget(
        containerId,
        tenant,
        storyId,
        widgetId,
        {
        filters: [{
          model: "sap.epm.Planned_Events_Sample:Planned_Events_Sample",
          values: [{
            dimension: "Date_5x2a25042z",
            value: ["026b7dcf2350c824559b8cc00612de","05aff8031d1c560fa0ea6a675cc894","53a4b48d6e946faa59c35643d775c9"],
            operation: 'in'
          }]
          },       
             {
            model:"sap.epm.Planned_Events_Sample:Planned_Events_Sample",
           values: [{
           dimension: "Event_672z323k1f",
           value: [""],
           operation: 'notIn'
          }]
        },
        {
            model:"sap.epm.Planned_Events_Sample:Planned_Events_Sample",
           values: [{
             dimension: "Date_5x2a25042z",
           value: ["026b7dcf2350c824559b8cc00612de"],
           operation: 'notIn'
          }]
        }
      ] 
      },
updatedOptions
      )
    };

  8. Troubleshooting Tips

  • Chart not loading? Ensure the SAC tenant URL is correct and accessible from the web app.
  • CORS errors? Verify that the SAC instance allows CORS from your application domain.
  • Authentication issues? Confirm the valid authentication or SSO setup is correctly configured.

  9. Further Reading & Resources

  • Enabling Iframe Embedding
  • SAP Widget API Documentation
